journal-title-group.journal-title: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research

title-group.article-title: A Comparison of the Cystotome-Assisted Prechop and Phaco-Chop Phacoemulsification Nucleotomy Techniques

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Ophthalmology, Eye, Ear, Nose and Throat (Eye and ENT) Hospital of Fudan University, Shanghai, P.R. China
2Shanghai Aier Eye Hospital, Aier School of Ophthalmology, Central South University, Shanghai, P.R. China
3Harbin Aier Eye Hospital, Aier School of Ophthalmology, Central South University, Harbin, Heilongjiang, P.R. China
4Shenyang Aier Eye Hospital, Aier School of Ophthalmology, Central South University, Shenyang, Liaoning, P.R. China

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author: Xiao-Ling An, e-mail: eyeanxiaoling@163.com

fn.p: Lian-Qun Wu and Xu Chen contributed equally to this work and should be considered as equal first authors

license.license-p: This work is licensed under Creative Common Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-ND 4.0)

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The aim of this study was to compare the intra-operative parameters and post-operative outcomes of cataract surgery performed using the cystotome-assisted prechop (CAP) and phaco-chop techniques.

sec.title: Material/Methods
sec.p: Fifty-two eyes with age-related cataract in the CAP group, and 63 eyes in the phaco-chop group were enrolled for analysis in this study, and the surgical outcomes were reported 1 day and/or 1 week, and 1 month post-operatively.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The CAP technique was associated with statistically significantly lower cumulative dissipated energy compared with the phaco-chop technique (P<0.001). The mean endothelial cell loss in the CAP group was statistically significantly lower than that of the phaco-chop group 1 week (5.6±5.9% versus 8.8±8.7%, P=0.020) and 1 month post-operatively (6.3±6.8% versus 9.8±9.9%, P=0.026). The change in the central corneal thickness between the 2 groups was significantly different at 1 day post-operatively (3.3±3.1% versus 4.9±4.6%, P=0.036). The change in the 8.0 mm central corneal volume between the 2 groups was significantly different at 1 day and 1 week post-operatively (6.5±6.1% versus 10.9±7.9%, P=0.001; 3.2±4.7% versus 5.4±5.7%, P=0.029, respectively).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The CAP technique showed lower ultrasound energy consumption and less endothelial damage and corneal edema than the phaco-chop technique. It might therefore prove a cost-effective prechop method for cataract surgery.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Advances in cataract surgery techniques have provided various options for the reduction of the size of the incision and the phacoemulsification energy, which could improve the clinical outcomes [1,2]. The prechop technique, which was first reported by Akahoshi in 1998, revolutionized the chop technique popularly used in cataract surgery [3]. Many prechop techniques use special instruments to fracture the nucleus manually instead of grooving or sculpting, which decrease the ultrasound energy delivered during the cataract surgery and reduce the damage to the corneal endothelium and other intraocular structures consequently. In 2015, Chen et al. described a manual prechop technique named the cystotome-assisted prechop (CAP) technique which fragment the nucleus in the sac by using a surgeon-bent cystotome and a Nagahara chopper [4]. In this study, we intend to perform a comparison of the intraoperative energy consumption and clinical outcomes between the CAP and conventional phaco-chop phacoemulsification nucleotomy techniques in a prospective series of phacoemulsification surgeries.

sec.title: Patients
sec.p: Patients with age-related cataract underwent cataract surgeries using CAP or phaco-chop phacoemulsification nucleotomy techniques were enrolled in this study from Aier Eye Hospital, Harbin, China, from March 9, 2015 to March 26, 2015. The grading of the cataract was evaluated based on the Lens Opacities Classification System III (LOCS III) by the same physician [5]. LOCS III classifies cataracts as nuclear color (NC), nuclear opalescence (NO), cortical (C), and posterior subcapsular (P) cataracts, and NO is graded to 6 degrees based on a group of 6 standardized slit-lamp images of nuclear cataracts [5]. Eyes with NO graded between 2 and 4 were included for analysis in this study. The exclusion criteria included a history of ocular trauma or ophthalmic surgery, ocular comorbidities, corneal disorders and an endothelial cell count (ECC) fewer than 1500 cells/mm2. Patients without regular follow-up were excluded as well. This study adhered to the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. The institutional review board of Aier Eye Hospital Group approved both the clinical protocol and the consent forms used in the study. Informed consent was obtained from each patient.

sec.title: Patient evaluation
sec.p: All patients received a full ophthalmic examination, which included the best-corrected distance visual acuity, manifest refraction, slit-lamp examination, intraocular pressure measurement (NT-2000, Nidek Co., Ltd.), central corneal thickness (CCT) and central corneal volume (CCV) by a non-contact rotating Scheimpflug camera (Pentacam, Oculus Optikgeräte GmbH), ECC by a non-contact specular microscope (SP-3000 P, Topcon, Tokyo), biometry by A-mode ultrasound (Cinescan, Quantel Medical) or coherence interferometry (IOLMaster, Carl Zeiss Meditec AG) and fundus examination. All patients underwent the same pre-operative standardized management.

sec.title: Phaco machine and settings
sec.p: The Infiniti® phacoemulsification unit (Alcon Laboratories, Fort Worth, TX, USA) and the 0.9 mm mini-flared, 45-degree ABS Kelman microtip were used for each cataract surgery in this study. The settings of the phaco machine were as follows: the torsional continual model with 100% amplitude, infusion bottle height of 100 cm, aspiration flow rate of 35 mL/min, and vacuum 400 mm Hg. The phaco machine settings used for the CAP and phaco-chop groups were identical.

sec.title: Surgical technique
sec.p: All the surgeries were performed under topical anesthesia by an experienced surgeon (XLA), who previously used the CAP technique in more than 500 cases and performed more than 10 000 cataract surgeries prior to this study. Each cataract surgery involved a 3.2 mm limbal incision, and a 5.5–6.0 mm continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis using a 26-gauge needle, but no hydrodisection.

For the phaco-chop technique which has been reported widely, in brief, the phaco tip stabilized the nucleus and then the Nagahara chopper cleaved and pulled apart nucleus into 2 halves. Then, this process was repeated to break up the both nucleus halves into small pieces. At last, the nucleus fragments were removed by the phacoemulsification handpiece.

For the CAP technique, the following procedures have been reported previously by Chen et al. [4]. Here is a brief introduction. Firstly, a bent needle tip instead of the phaco tip was used to stabilize the nucleus, whilst the Nagahara chopper was placed at the lens equator. Then the bent needle and the chopper were brought together in the center to split the nucleus into 2 halves, which was almost the same as that used in the phaco-chop technique. Then the same chopping procedures were used to fragment both hemispheres into small pieces which were finally removed by the ultrasound energy (Videos 1 and 2).

Subsequently, the procedures were identical for both CAP and phaco-chop techniques. The remaining epinucleus and cortex were removed completely, and intraocular lenses were implanted into capsular bag. Post-operatively, each patient received tobramycin/dexamethasone eye drops (TobraDex, Alcon Laboratories, Fort Worth, TX, USA) 4 times a day for the first 2 weeks, and pranoprofen eye drops (Pranopulin, Senju Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Japan) for the following 1 month.

sec.title: Outcome assessment
sec.p: The mean cumulative dissipated energy (CDE) of each cataract surgery was recorded intraoperatively. The mean CDE power represents the mean percentage of power consumed during the ultrasound time, which is equal to the mean ultrasound power multiplied by the ultrasound time [6].

The ECC was measured 1 day prior to surgery, as well as 1 week and 1 month after surgery. The endothelial cell density was calculated with the IMAGE net software (Topcon). At each patient visit, 3 photographs per eye were taken. The mean value of the 3 results was used for analysis. The CCT and CCV within 8.0 mm circles were measured 1 day before surgery, as well as 1 day, 1 week, and 1 month post-operatively. Three readings were obtained and averaged. All the ophthalmic examinations were performed by the same technician.

The percentage of endothelial cell loss was calculated as follows: Loss of ECC (%)=(ECCpre–ECCpost)/ECCpre. Pre is short for pre-operative and post is short for post-operative. The change in CCT was calculated as follows: Change in CCT (%)=(CCTpost–CCTpre)/CCTpre. The change in CCV was calculated by the same formula as that used to measure the change in CCT.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Data distribution and homogeneity were analyzed. Normally distributed variables were expressed as mean±standard deviation. The independent samples t-test was used to compare the mean values between the 2 groups, whereas paired t-test was used to compare the pre-operative and post-operative data. A chi-square test was applied to analyze the qualitative data. The correlations between the variables were examined using Spearman’s rank correlation test. A P<0.05 was considered statistically significant (SPSS 19.0; IBM, Chicago, IL, USA).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Two patients in the CAP group and 3 in the phaco-chop group were excluded because of loss to follow-up, and 115 eyes of 115 patients (52 eyes in the CAP group, and 63 eyes in the phaco-chop group) were enrolled for analysis in this study. The demographic and clinical features of the CAP and phaco-chop groups are presented in Table 1. No significant differences in gender, age and NO were observed between the 2 groups. Furthermore, no significant differences in the mean ECC (Table 2), CCT (Table 3) and 8.0 mm CCV (Table 4) pre-operatively were found between the 2 groups. All the surgeries were uneventful.

sec.title: Cumulative dissipated energy
sec.p: The mean CDE was significantly lower in the CAP group (13.69±4.51) than in the phaco-chop group (20.31±6.13) (P<0.001, independent samples t-test); therefore, the CDE was reduced by 32.59% more in the CAP group than in the phaco-chop group. For each NO grade, the mean CDE was lower in the CAP group than in the phaco-chop group, although the differences were only statistically significant in the NO3 and NO4 subgroups (Table 5).

sec.title: Endothelial cell count
sec.p: In both CAP and phaco-chop groups, the ECCs at 1 week and 1 month post-operatively were significantly less than the pre-operative data (P<0.001 for both, paired t-test). The percentage of endothelial cell loss in phaco-chop group was statistically significantly higher than that in CAP group at 1 week (P=0.020, Table 2) and 1 month (P=0.026, Table 2) after surgery, respectively. No statistically significant differences in the absolute ECCs throughout the follow-up (P=0.374 at 1 week and P=0.341 at 1 month, respectively, Table 2) were observed between the 2 groups. The losses of ECC at 1 week and 1 month post-operatively were positively correlated with the nucleus grade (r=0.430, P<0.001 and r=0.389, P<0.001, respectively) and the CDE (r=0.432, P<0.001 and r=0.378, P<0.001, respectively).

sec.title: Central corneal thickness
sec.p: The mean CCT increased significantly in both the CAP and phaco-chop groups at 1 day (both P<0.001) and 1 week (both P<0.001) post-operatively, and returned to the pre-operative levels at 1 month after surgery (P=0.561 and P=0.091, respectively). The change in CCT from the pre-operative values at 1 day post-operatively was less in the CAP group than in the phaco-chop group (P=0.036, Table 3). No significant difference in absolute CCT at 1 day, 1 week and 1 month post-operatively was observed between the CAP and phaco-chop groups (Table 3). Significant positive correlations were found between the changes in CCT at 1 day, 1 week and 1 month post-operatively and the nucleus grade (r=0.320, P<0.001, r=0.336, P<0.001, and r=0.378, P<0.001, respectively) and the CDE (r=0.246, P=0.008, r=0.207, P=0.026, and r=0.204, P=0.029, respectively).

sec.title: Central corneal volume
sec.p: Compared with the pre-operative values, the 8.0 mm CCV increased significantly in both the CAP and phaco-chop groups at 1 day (both P<0.001), 1 week (both P<0.001) and 1 month (P=0.001 and P<0.001, respectively) post-operatively. The change in the 8.0 mm CCV between the 2 groups was statistically significantly different at 1 day and 1 week post-operatively (P=0.001 and P=0.029, respectively, Table 4). A significant difference in the absolute 8.0 mm CCV between the CAP and phaco-chop groups was observed at 1 day post-operatively (P=0.037, Table 4), although no significant difference was found at 1 week and 1 month post-operatively (P=0.416 and P=0.605, respectively, Table 4). Significant positive correlations were observed between the changes in the 8.0 mm CCV at 1 day, 1 week and 1 month post-operatively and the nucleus grade (r=0.384, P<0.001, r=0.431, P<0.001, and r=0.439, P<0.001, respectively) and the CDE (r=0.510, P<0.001, r=0.521, P<0.001, and r=0.423, P<0.001, respectively).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: After cataract surgery, the endothelial cell loss is significantly higher than the physiological rate, and it is therefore considered an important parameter for estimating the safety of the utilized surgical technique [7]. The percentage of endothelial cell loss associated with phacoemulsification varies considerably [8]. For instance, Pirazzoli et al. showed that the endothelial cell loss at 8 weeks post-operatively was 4.72±1.2% in the phaco-chop group and 13.80±4.3% in the divide and conquer phaco-fracture group [9]. Storr-Paulsen et al. reported that the mean endothelial cell loss was 173 cells/mm2 (6.3%) at 3 months post-operatively in the phaco-chop group after surgery using a Storz Millennium phacoemulsification machine with a Kelman-type microtip (30 degree) and standardized machine settings [10]. Furthermore, Liu et al. reported a mean 10.4% and 12.5% reduction in endothelial cells at 7 days and 30 days post-operatively, respectively, after phacoemulsification using the standard quick-chop technique with the Infiniti® Vision System and the torsional continual mode [6]. In the present study, the mean endothelial cell losses were 8.8% at 1 week and 9.8% at 1 month post-operatively in the phaco-chop group, which was in accordance with the findings of previous reports [6].

Endothelial cell loss could be affected by several factors, including an advanced age, small pupil diameter, high nucleus grade, and a greater amount of total emitted ultrasound energy [11]. In this study, the CAP group was associated with a significantly lower rate of endothelial cell loss, compared with the phaco-chop group, at both 1 week (5.6% versus 8.8%, P=0.020) and 1 month (6.3% versus 9.8%, P=0.026) post-operatively. The correlation analysis showed that the losses of endothelial cells were positively correlated with both the nucleus grade and the CDE. No significant differences in either age or NO were observed between the CAP and phaco-chop groups; therefore, the total ultrasound energy may be responsible for the difference in the rate of endothelial cell loss between the 2 groups. The CDE was lower in the CAP group than in the phaco-chop group, especially in the sub-groups with nucleus grades 3 and 4. Studies concerning other chopping techniques, such as the phaco rolling technique, phaco 1-chop nucleotomy technique, phaco slice and separate technique, and karate prechop technique, similarly found that they could reduce the phaco time and/or energy [12–15]. The phaco prechop technique, including karate prechopping and counter prechopping, could significantly reduce the effective ultrasound time (P=0.033), as well as the 3-month post-operative endothelial cell loss (13.86% vs. 17.72%, P=0.001), compared with the divide and conquer technique used in cataract surgery [16]. Furthermore, Abell and colleagues reported an 83.6% reduction in the mean effective phaco time (EPT) and a 36.1% reduction in the endothelial cell loss in the femtosecond pre-treatment group compared with the conventional phacoemulsification group [17].

The energy and time associated with phacoemulsification are considered the 2 most important risk factors for corneal endothelial cell loss, as well as ocular structural damage [18]. In this study, a significant reduction in the changes in CCT and CCV was observed following cataract surgery performed using the CAP technique compared with the phaco-chop technique. The correlation analysis showed that the changes in CCT and CCV during the first post-operative month were positively correlated with the CDE. These results suggested that manually prechopping the nucleus into pieces by using the CAP technique helped reduce the total ultrasound energy and thus corneal edema. These results were in accordance with those reported in relation to femtosecond laser-assisted cataract surgery (FLACS) [19]. A meta-analysis reported that FLACS significantly reduced the mean phaco energy and EPT required in the surgery, whilst the CCT was significantly lower in the FLACS group at 1 day post-operatively, compared with the conventional phacoemulsification cataract surgery group [19]. In this study, the CCT increased after cataract surgery, although it returned to pre-operative levels at 1 month post-operatively. However, the 8.0 mm CCV remained elevated past the first post-operative month. This result might be due to the persistent edema of the peripheral cornea around the incision. Similar results were reported in previous studies [20,21]. Wong et al. showed that the 5.0 and 7.0 mm CCVs remained elevated at 1 month post-operatively [20], whereas Suzuki et al. found that the 10.0 mm CCV remained significantly higher, even at 3 months post-operatively [21]. These results support the conclusion that CCV measurements, including the more peripheral cornea, offer more reliable data than the CCT on corneal edema following cataract surgery.

No intraoperative or post-operative complications in either the CAP group or the phaco-chop group were reported, and the clinical results were highly satisfactory. The most common complications associated with the CAP technique are capsule rupture and dehiscence of the zonular fibers when the surgery is performed by inexperienced surgeons. However, the learning curve for the CAP technique might actually be shorter than people anticipate because many surgeons are already familiar with the techniques of needle capsulorhexis and the horizontal chop technique. As we previously described, the CAP technique uses a bent needle tip instead of the phaco tip, whilst the chopping maneuver is almost the same as that used in the phaco-chop technique [4]. In addition, the length of the bent needle tip is about 1 mm, which never exceeds the thickness of the central lens. The key points in relation to performing a successful CAP procedure are the proper positioning of the Nagahara chopper and the needle tip, as well as the appropriate direction of the chopping force [4]. When incomplete chops occur, converting the CAP procedure into the traditional phaco-chop technique is easy. Recently, Qi replaced the needle used in the CAP technique with newly invented forceps that were patented in China in 2016 for both capsulorhexis and mechanical nuclear prechopping. This modification of the CAP technique would make the process more convenient for surgeons who are familiar with performing capsulorhexis with forceps, and, therefore, would help shorten their learning curve for the CAP technique. In our experience, it is best for beginners to choose moderate-density nuclei cases. Contraindications of CAP technique include brunescent and black cataracts, white cataracts. and cases with zonular dialysis.

FLACS is one of the most recent advances in cataract removal techniques, and it offers several potential advantages with regard to corneal incision creation, capsulotomy and reduced ultrasound energy consumption, compared with traditional phacoemulsification cataract surgery [2,22]. However, the relatively high cost of FLACS systems limits the widespread application of this type of surgery, especially in developing countries [22,23]. The CAP technique is an efficient and economical prechop method for cataract removal that does not require a special or expensive instrument. For this reason, a prospective, randomized trial comparing the efficacy and safety of the CAP technique with those of the femtosecond laser-assisted lens fragmentation technique or other prechop methods is warranted in patients with different nucleus grades.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The results of the present study are encouraging. The CAP technique was found to be associated with less CDE, less endothelial cell loss and milder corneal edema during the early post-operative period, compared with the phaco-chop technique. It might be a good alternative for cataract surgery.

fn.p: Source of support: The Fundamental Research Funds of China Hunan Provincial Science&Technology Department (2017SK50904). The funds of Shanghai Changning Medical Science and Technology Planning Project (CNKW2016Y30). National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81600765). The Foundation of Shanghai Municipal Commission of Health and Family Planning (Project No. 201640120)

caption.p: A case of age-related cataract (nuclear opalescence graded 4) performed using the cystotome-assisted prechop technique.
A case of age-related cataract (nuclear opalescence graded 2) performed using the cystotome-assisted prechop technique.
Comparison of clinical profile of CAP and phaco-chop groups include brunescent CAP, cystotome-assisted prechop.
Comparison of corneal endothelial cells pre-operatively and post-operatively in CAP and phaco-chop groups.

fn.p: CAP – cystotome-assisted prechop; ECC – endothelial cell count; 95% CI – 95% confidence interval.

caption.p: Comparison of central corneal thickness pre-operatively and post-operatively in CAP and phaco-chop group.

fn.p: CAP – cystotome-assisted prechop; CCT – central corneal thickness; 95% CI – 95% confidence interval.

caption.p: Comparison of central corneal volume pre-operatively and post-operatively in CAP and phaco-chop groups.

fn.p: CAP – cystotome-assisted prechop; CCV – central corneal volume; 95% CI – 95% confidence interval.

caption.p: Comparison of cumulative dissipated energy in CAP and phaco-chop groups.
